abstract: This tutorial review examines the design, synthesis, and host–guest chemistry of discrete coordination cages formed through the self-assembly of banana-shaped, bis-monodentate pyridyl ligands and square-planar metal cations, specifically Pd(II) and Pt(II). While [M₂L₄] stoichiometry represents the most common structural motif for these assemblies, the review explores how variations in ligand geometry, flexibility, and environmental factors can lead to more complex architectures, including [M₃L₆] and [M₄L₈] rings, intricate double-trefoil knots, and interpenetrated double-cages. Because these structures are typically cationic, they exhibit a high affinity for anionic guests, often demonstrating significant selectivity based on the size and shape of the internal cavities. Detailed discussions are provided on advanced host–guest phenomena such as the linear stacking of metal complexes, positive cooperativity in allosteric binding, and the development of stimuli-responsive systems. Finally, the review highlights the integration of functional, redox-active, or photoswitchable backbones into the ligand design, facilitating the light-controlled uptake and release of molecular cargo and paving the way for applications in molecular machinery and artificial catalysis.
